sanskrit:
य एनं वेत्ति हन्तारं यश्चैनं मन्यते हतम्।
उभौ तौ न विजानीतो नायं हन्ति न हन्यते॥
transliteration:
ya enaṁ vetti hantāraṁ yaś cainaṁ manyate hatam
ubhau tau na vijānīto nāyaṁ hanti na hanyate
literal_meaning: Anyone who knows this (self) as the killer, and anyone who thinks this (self) is killed—both of them do not understand; this (self) neither kills nor is killed.
contextual_meaning: Krishna confronts Arjuna’s existential dread by reframing violence: the true self is an indestructible witness that transcends both agency and victimhood, dissolving guilt and fear of annihilation. Psychologically, it invites the warrior (and the modern user) to disidentify from the egoic narrative of being either perpetrator or casualty, revealing a deathless core that remains untouched by action or consequence.
psychological_state: A person paralyzed by the belief that their necessary action will irreversibly damage both others and their own moral self-image.
trigger_scenario: A surgeon who must amputate to save a life yet feels they are ‘maiming’ the patient, or a CEO laying off staff to keep the company alive while haunted by the thought ‘I am destroying them.’
anchor_text: You are the unbroken witness; no action can slay you.
